summary: Taylor Donskey is a player friend of mine who lives in the cities and is about my age. I know he teaches at Twin Town Guitars, which is a well-known place around here. He's part of the younger generation of musicians I'm trying to get perspectives from in this oral history project. Our connection is through music and the local scene in Minnesota.

Twin Town Guitars is where Taylor Donskey teaches, and it's a place I've heard of in the local music community. It’s part of the fabric of the music scene here in the Twin Cities. I’ve been involved with people connected to it, and it represents a hub for music education and guitar players in the area. It’s one of the organizations I referenced when discussing the impact of the pandemic on music teaching.

Chris Bowlby is someone I studied with at the University of St. Thomas. He's part of the group of musicians and educators I’m gathering perspectives from in this oral history project. Chris represents the academic side of the music world, and I’ve known him personally through our studies. His experience adds a valuable viewpoint on how the pandemic has affected music education and performance.
cues: studied with at University of St. Thomas

summary: The University of St. Thomas is where I studied alongside people like Chris Bowlby. It’s a place that shaped part of my musical education and network. The university represents the academic environment for music in Minnesota, and it’s part of the broader context of how educators and students have been adapting to the pandemic. I’ve reflected on how adjunct professors there, like Pete, have had to navigate new challenges during this time.

Chris Keishon is someone I know personally and included in this oral history project to get a wider variety of perspectives. He’s part of the local music community and represents another voice among young people involved in music programming and academia. I mentioned him alongside Taylor and Chris Bowlby as part of the group contributing to this conversation about music during the pandemic.

Hell's Kitchen is where I played my last public jazz gig on March 11, right before everything shut down due to the pandemic. It was a great gig with Phil, and it felt like the last moment of normalcy before the venues closed. That place was part of my regular gig calendar, and losing those live venues was a huge blow to musicians like me. It’s a key location in my story of how the pandemic abruptly changed my performance life.

The Lexington in Saint Paul was one of my regular gig spots before the pandemic hit. I used to play there frequently as part of my busy schedule, which could be five to eight gigs a week. When the pandemic forced venues to close, The Lexington was one of many places that shut down, which meant a sudden stop to my live performances. It’s part of the network of venues that supported my career in the Twin Cities area.

Midtown Global Market was another venue where I regularly performed before the pandemic. It’s a vibrant place in the Twin Cities that hosts live music, and I was part of that scene. Like other venues, it closed during the pandemic, contributing to the sudden halt in live performances. It’s a place I miss playing at and that’s representative of the local music ecosystem.

I used to play at a place in White Bear Lake regularly before the pandemic shut everything down. It was part of my steady gig calendar along with other venues in the area. White Bear Lake represents one of the suburban or outlying spots where I connected with audiences. The loss of these gigs was part of the broader impact on my performance schedule in 2020.

Ingredients Cafe was one of the places I played regularly before the pandemic. It’s part of the local venues that supported live jazz and other music. When the pandemic hit, Ingredients Cafe was among the venues that closed, which meant losing a key part of my performance income and community. It’s a reminder of how widespread the impact was on musicians in the Twin Cities.

The Dakota Jazz Club is a well-known venue where I used to perform regularly. It’s a staple in the Minneapolis music scene and was part of my regular gig calendar before the pandemic. Like other venues, it closed during the shutdown, which was a big loss for musicians and audiences alike. I feel fortunate to have had busy periods there before everything stopped.

Cadenza is the music school where I taught many students before the pandemic forced us to move online. The owner, Dan, is a good friend, and we worked together to transition lessons to a virtual format. It was challenging at first, especially with the tech side, but I managed to keep teaching my students there remotely. Cadenza represents a community and support system that helped me continue my work during this difficult time.

Amazon was crucial for me during the pandemic because I ordered a tech interface from them to improve my online teaching setup. They delivered it quickly, which helped me get my lessons running more smoothly after a rough start. Without Amazon’s fast shipping, I might have struggled longer with the technical problems of remote teaching. It’s an example of how technology and services helped musicians adapt during the crisis.

Pete is someone I know who was at Macalester College, and I believe he was an adjunct professor there. We talked about how adjunct educators like him have had to figure out the new realities of teaching online during the pandemic. His experience reflects the challenges faced by many educators who had to quickly acquire new tech skills without much institutional support. Pete’s situation helped me think about the broader responsibilities placed on teachers in this crisis.

Macalester College is where Pete teaches as an adjunct professor, and it represents the higher education environment affected by the pandemic. The college, like many institutions, had to shift to online learning, which created new challenges for faculty. I’ve reflected on how adjuncts there, including Pete, had to quickly learn technology to keep teaching. Macalester is part of the academic landscape in Minnesota that’s adapting to these changes.

Dan is the owner of Cadenza, the music school where I teach. He’s a good friend and was working hard to manage the chaos of moving lessons online during the pandemic. Dan’s leadership helped keep the school running and supported instructors like me through the transition. His role was crucial in helping us adapt and continue teaching despite the challenges.

Ryan is one of my younger students who adapted really well to online lessons during the pandemic. He performs regularly and wasn’t fazed by the virtual format, which made teaching him easier. Ryan’s experience shows how younger musicians are more comfortable with technology and can even benefit from the focused nature of online lessons. He’s a good example of how some students thrived despite the challenges.

Krissy Bergmark is a tabla player from the Twin Cities who I consider a cool and positive musician. I watched her perform in one of the first Zoom concerts about a month ago. She’s part of the local music community adapting to new ways of performing during the pandemic. Her online performance was a memorable example of how musicians are staying connected with audiences virtually.

Liz Draper is a bass player I’ve worked with and who also performed in the same Zoom concert as Krissy Bergmark. I know her from the local music scene, and seeing her in that online performance was inspiring. Liz represents the collaborative spirit of musicians who are finding ways to keep performing despite the pandemic. Her participation in virtual concerts highlights the resilience of our community.

Venmo came up in conversations about how musicians and educators are managing payments during the pandemic. It’s one of the platforms people use to receive money for lessons or gigs when traditional venues are closed. While I didn’t go into detail, Venmo represents part of the financial ecosystem that artists rely on in this new environment. It’s a tool that helps keep transactions flowing in uncertain times.

PayPal is another payment service mentioned in the context of how musicians and teachers handle money during the pandemic. Like Venmo, it helps facilitate transactions when in-person payments aren’t possible. I recognize PayPal as part of the infrastructure that supports independent contractors and educators like myself. It’s essential for maintaining income streams when traditional venues and methods are disrupted.
